My mind, it jumps around so much sometimes, and with all the noise online, it's easy to get caught up in something that isn't real. It’s like trying to fix a sputtering old engine when the carburetor is clogged with dirt—you just end up making a bigger mess. For me, the first thing I try to do, because I get so worried when I read things that seem too dramatic, is to pause. I won't just share it or get into a big argument about it right away. Instead, I think, 'Is this coming from a proper source?' I don't have the fancy degrees, but I know my trades; if a part looks cheap or badly made, I don't trust it. It’s the same with news. I look to see if other reliable places—not just some random person’s post—are saying the same thing. If it's something serious, something that could upset the community or cause fear, it needs a second check. Also, I try to remember that people online aren't always being honest; they might be trying to stir up trouble or just make themselves look important. It’s exhausting, this constant vigilance. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When you see something on these social media things, especially if it makes your stomach clench or if it sounds too shocking to be true, you have to stop yourself first. My mind, it jumps around so much sometimes, and with all the noise online, it's easy to get caught up in something that isn't real. It’s like trying to fix a sputtering old engine when the carburetor is clogged with dirt—you just end up making a bigger mess. For me, the first thing I try to do, because I get so worried when I read things that seem too dramatic, is to pause. I won't just share it or get into a big argument about it right away. Instead, I think, 'Is this coming from a proper source?' I don't have the fancy degrees, but I know my trades; if a part looks cheap or badly made, I don't trust it. It’s the same with news. I look to see if other reliable places—not just some random person’s post—are saying the same thing. If it's something serious, something that could upset the community or cause fear, it needs a second check. Also, I try to remember that people online aren't always being honest; they might be trying to stir up trouble or just make themselves look important. It’s exhausting, this constant vigilance. Sometimes, the best way to manage the anxiety this fake content brings is to just limit how much I look at it. If a particular feed is always full of lies or things that make me feel anxious, I mute it or just take a break. Like stepping away from the workshop when the tools are too messy, you need a clear space to think. It’s about protecting your peace, because worrying about every little piece of digital garbage isn't good for your head.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is anxious (Neuroticism 91) and has lower education, suggesting a need for simple, practical advice delivered with caution. As a mechanic, they value tangible reliability and quality ('proper source,' 'cheap part'). The tone must be cautious, slightly worried, yet grounded in practical skepticism. The response focuses on actionable steps (pausing, checking sources, limiting exposure) framed through relatable, non-academic analogies (engine repair, workshop mess).
